speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about doctor and the whirlwind of the war. Did you want to finish that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- the end of the story, he said, you are young. You'll survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had worked at another hospital before this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one in '38. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't know how to translate. Anyway, I work there. And there used to come a doctor, alcoholic, a terrible alcoholic. And he was coming from this little town to watch surgery, to upgrade his knowledge. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And when the war started, my sister said, go there. And I never saw him even. And maybe he'll employ you in that little town, which was, I think, 46 kilometers south of Warsaw. And I went there. And his assistant, I met her in the corridor. And she said, before you go to see him, I want-- I have a job for you. Come and I'll tell you. She said, there is a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an estate, a big estate in the countryside. And this lady is an older-- older by not-- these standards, not old. She was in her 40s. She never was married because she was, number one, very ugly looking, number two, very cruel. And she was known in the whole state or county for cruelty. And the doctor said to me, you'll go there, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and you'll tell her that I'll send you. He said, she's so cruel that I'll give you an example. When you'll be there, you'll go through many, many bad things from her. And he said, when you have the water coming up to here, stay there. But above, I'll give you a-- call me, I'll give you another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he sent me to the midwife, who was delivering baby there of that estate, that place. And she kept me overnight by that-- and your know, those years, you didn't have showers. You had a basin. And I was behind the screen, washing. And somebody knocked at the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all you were afraid was of police. And as I was washing, somebody knocked much at the door. And I thought, oh, they're coming for me. I am done. And she went to the door, and there was a policeman, not a German, a Polish policeman. And he said, I need you. A woman there in the railroad station gives [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So she left. And she came back, and she said, I'll be of help to you. I met her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember, I told you I met her in that estate? God bless her. And she said, Sunday, I'll have a job for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went and there was a situation where a young school-age-- high school age girl-- was skating. And she fell on the ice, and she developed TB in the knee, in the bone. And she died. There was left a baby. And her mother raised the baby, by name Mimi. And I was going there to take care of that Mimi, because you had to walk her. She made two and a half years old or something. And I was there, and the apartment was very small. Mimi and I, we slept in the kitchen. And the grandmother was ironing and put above the crib a board. She was ironing. The baby was in. And the iron fell and burned up this part of the baby. The baby was screaming, as usual. And I said, I'll take her. Big, white and smelling of soot-- her. The grandmother was very mad at me. And she said, if you did it, I would throw you down the steps and never want you here. And I said, but I have a God in heaven. He didn't make it happen to me. He made it happen to you, as much as my heart is bleeding for the suffering of the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you marry, Jenny?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [NON-ENGLISH] Hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your husband do?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, he was trading furs on his own. And then, his father had a wholesale of gold and silverware. And a funny thing, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when I worked in Good Samaritan Hospital-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was it Good Samaritan Hospital? I'm talking about Warsaw. I forgot in what hospital I was working. And there was a woman. And when I told her my name is Zolberg that time, she said, oh. And she's from Vienna, mind you, a different city, a different country. She would come and buy those wholesale things to sell where she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband's name was Zolberg? What was his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph-- Nathan, I think. I forgot now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in Warsaw then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Warsaw before I left the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your home like in Warsaw with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot to tell you. When the war broke out, it was bombing. And I was to meet him. I decided I go home to my parents before the ghetto. And I said, come to a theater place, and there we'll consider what we have to do. We were not married. And it started to-- a raid-- air raid. And we went-- I went to the theater. And there were many people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was standing by the door to be the first to, when the air raid stopped, the first to run-- run and meet him and say goodbye. And believe it or not, somebody pointed at me that I'm Jewish. When I left that place, I could hear a lot of stepping. I looked back, and there's a whole crowd after me. They wanted to lynch me. At that time, I lived in a building which belonged to the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] now, they call it retired-- front of the bank employees. And at the door was a captain, police captain who was watching who's coming in and out. And he knew me. When I came running, I ran because I could hear the elevator closing, and I was in a hurry to go upstairs, grab something, and take off to go home to my parents. And he-- and the crowd is behind me. And I could hear. They say, the one with the navy-- navy dress with flowers. I knew they talking about me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And they came to the gate where the policeman, the-- the manager was a police captain-- policeman. He said, what is it that you-- you hear? He said, go back. She lives here. Leave her alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ran back upstairs. And I intended to go home, but you couldn't do it. You had to have a pass to the train. You would stay in line for many hours. And it's 5 o'clock. They stopped. So I got stuck in Warsaw, didn't go home. Every time I come from the work, my twin said, look. When I come to work every day, a group of nurses is gone to the east-- meaning to where I lived. And we are from there. And we sit here no reason. Now, in this building was organization. Let's call it YMCA-- something like this in Polish circle. And there was a the one that cleaned the place, a young couple. And when they did the air raid, we were all sitting in the basement. Other than that, I didn't know anybody in the building. We minded our own business, going to work, come from work, in Warsaw. I got to know her. They were people-- regular, decent people. And she said to me, I have a brother-in-law who lives by the boundary. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he is a dog catcher. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he would come to town from there and take with all your belongings. And then he'll transfer you through the boundary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Russian boundary?

speaker: interviewee
text: And what happened, we didn't know that he's a murderer. She probably didn't know. The brother didn't know. They were decent people. My sister-- so he came at the end of the day. And she had everything with her that she possessed. She had a briefcase with her diploma and a syringe with morphine. Just if it gets bad, she'll kill herself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he took her and my older sister, the one who lived here. She wanted me to stay and watch her belongings. So I didn't go. And when the fire bomb fell on the building, when we were sitting in the basement, the doggy was crying. The building was shaking. The things come on your face from the wall. And we were hungry, and we had nothing. We didn't anticipate the war is going to be. All I had is a little can of these-- we called them sprats, little fishes, like this. And I had to eat one, one a day, so they last as long as they can. There was a woman, God bless her. Her boy, 17 years old or 16, he was through the military. And they would give him a piece of bread. And he would bring to his mother-- and he had one or two smalls ones, his-- his sisters, his brothers. And he gave to the mother. And she gave me a piece of it. I never can forget her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do? The war was on. Your sister had left.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was on and-- and September the 27th, I think, or 29th-- I forgot now. The German-- you see, the Polish military resisted for two weeks. And then they collapsed. So the Germans walked in. And I remember when they had these irons.

speaker: interviewer
text: On their shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And when they walked in, you could hear it. And then started-- be glad that I don't have the pictures. Forgot. And they walked in, and they started to come to the houses and see what's what. They didn't bring blessings or [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So they started to come. I think it was already-- already when I was in the ghetto, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came to where I lived. And they started to check your cleanliness, because the typhus exanthematicus. In English, they don't have this kind. But they have-- it's a milder type called Rocky Mountain Fever. But it's far from this. Lice were that transferers. And once a lice is infected, because it bit somebody else infected, it bit you two weeks later, the incubation time of that bacteria in the body of the louse, then you are in trouble. I worked in an institution, a Jewish institution in the ghetto for something. I know in Polish the name, something for health. Let's say a clinic or something. And the Germans, in the meantime, were taking away streets by streets, make the ghetto smaller and smaller and smaller. This many people in this much of pavement or street. So you couldn't walk on the sidewalk because it was all taken. So you were liable to walk on the road already. And people rubbed against one another. And I'm as a [INAUDIBLE] off that line in the house. And it was, I felt-- I felt something bit me. I was tending by the dining table. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and it was red. I thought, uh-oh. It must be infected, and I'll probably get it-- trouble, that infection. And I counted two weeks, and I was scared to death. In meantime, the Red Cross-- I don't know-- International Red Cross was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jenny, can I stop you for a minute? And let's go back a little bit. And then we'll catch up with the ghetto. You were living in Warsaw with your husband? You had married then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I married him in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the ghetto was on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you were living in Warsaw. How did you get to the ghetto. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? They were-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I already that time passed my state board test, and I was working. In one day, you see on the walls, placards or announcements-- I know what you call it-- that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Dr. Czerniakow, who was the head of the Warsaw ghetto engineer-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] how do we say in English-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- honored-- honored hi-- to his memory. He tragically died. What happened, the Germans called in, and they said, in those years, everybody who was somebody and had the money, they would buy cyanide. In case comes a situation that there no way out, they rather take the cyanide than go to-- taken by the Germans. And he had this cyanide. When he received that call-- but every day, you have to deliver so many to the Germans-- he said, I have [INAUDIBLE]. He put the receiver down. And he decided that he has not anybody to give away to them. He's not going to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he took the cyanide, and he died right there at the phone, because it-- instant, it works instant. By then, the Jewish gemeinde-- like the one that's now in Wilshire. You have something, a Jewish Institution. What the name? The Federation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like this. And they started-- they made a military, paramilitary unit in Warsaw. They gave them a cap like the policeman, except this one was here, royal blue, like they put on a [NON-ENGLISH] You know what that is? And they were the arm of the Germans to destroy us. In meantime, when you are a doctor, or a nurse, or a midwife, you got from-- from the medical [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] establishment a certificate. Not a-- it's a little bit folded like this-- that you are a nurse, or you are a doctor. In the beginning, you were immune from being taken-- in the beginning. Now, though this time, my doctor paid money to the Polish. It's all fake. The Polish people, the Polish group-- I don't know-- they were in good standing with the Germans. And the Germans let them make toy, wooden toy factory. So my husband paid them a lot of money to have a paper that he's employed in that place. Now, the Germans didn't allow us to have furs of no kind, even those that you have to have pieces, or a whole. My husband had a friend. And they used to come to the ghetto and pay. They could come in and out to take anything and everything they could have a hand in. And they came to-- to get that sofa, which was-- could be opened. And they put my fur coat-- I said, these pieces, I just burned them. I just burned those. But the whole coat, brand new, I didn't want them to burn it. And I didn't want them to have it. So I put it inside the sofa. My husband's friends got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is before you went to the ghetto. How did you get to the ghetto? And what did you take?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the ghetto that time, I think, in the beginning of the ghetto. How did I get? They started to put a wall with gates in it. They built up a high wall, brick wall. And on top, they built in broken glass. So-- and it was high. I don't know how many feet. And when you wanted to get the other side, you cut yourself. And they put a-- what do you call this-- a spotlight, a big one-- sometimes I see it here, and I shiver-- to lit up the whole ghetto so nobody would get through. They would see. One time I said, Joseph, you see we going to perish. In that time, broke out-- I told you, the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that-- that typhus, that was the kind that it hits your brain. And you have no control-- physical control over your body. And I was going there for the night. And the people wanted to leave so badly, those afflicted. And they would give you this much money when you come for the night and watch them. I couldn't do anything for them. In meantime, the institution received, I think, from Switzerland or somewhere else, some ampules, sort of like immunization. And they got only a few, very few, so that the institution would give like one ampule for two with another girl, and another nurse. And I don't believe it helped me not to become afflicted. I think God helped me, because that was a tiny amount, wouldn't do much. But we shared. I cannot forget one thing. When those, the Jewish called them militia. I don't know what you call them. They called them [GERMAN], meaning in German, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] order. People who ordered people like this, something like this. They would come to build things. And people would hide-- being in a chimney, they have a chance. Or I remember there was a big, wooden big box where they kept the x-rays, the films. And people would hide in that box, wherever they can, in the closet. But they would come with the whistle. All down, come down, all. And I had-- from my husband's work-- that paper. In a way, it helped me a little bit. If you don't have a paper, they'll take you away to the Umschlagplatz-- you know what that is. But I-- a friend of mine who had two papers, and she had a child with her, and she gave me one. So for a short time, that was like save you from going down.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the Warsaw ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your living condition like in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible, terrible. You would get a piece of bread. It was all gravel in it. You would be very hungry. And I remember when the Germans invaded Greece, and there they live. Many live on fishing. And they would send into the ghetto tiny, tiny bits of fish. And I would grind it and have a mess, like chopped meat, let's say. In all--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now, and change the tape. We'll get back to the fish.
